message:
fix: connector_outbound_bridge zero-capacity, storage FTS rename, mux dispatch error propagation

Three independent fixes pulled into one commit because each is small
and orthogonal.

## 1. connector_outbound_bridge: fail-fast on dispatch_queue_capacity=0

ConnectorOutboundBridge::process_event previously compared

    self.dispatch_queue.len() >= self.config.dispatch_queue_capacity

to decide when to reject a dispatch. For `capacity == 0`, the
condition is always true because `len() >= 0` holds unconditionally
— so every action is blocked, which is correct semantically. BUT
the early-return path relied on the block happening inside the
already-populated `blocked` vec AFTER the capacity check; if a future
refactor reordered the vec-allocation vs the capacity check, a
capacity=0 config could accidentally allocate space for the blocked
action even though it had no chance of being dispatched.

Fix: add an explicit capacity==0 short-circuit BEFORE the queue-len
check:

    if self.config.dispatch_queue_capacity == 0 {
        self.telemetry.dispatch_queue_overflows += 1;
        warn!("outbound dispatch queue capacity is zero, blocking action");
        blocked.push(BlockedAction {
            reason: "dispatch_queue_full(capacity=0)".to_string(),
            ...
        });
        continue;
    }

Separate branch for the degenerate config so the telemetry counter
labels the failure class distinctly ("capacity=0" vs "queue_full
at capacity N"), making operators' "why is everything dropping"
dashboards clearer.

Doc comment on `actions_blocked` widened from "blocked by policy
or sandbox" to "blocked by policy, sandbox, or backpressure" since
the capacity=0 path is a backpressure drop, not a policy drop.

Regression test
`connector_outbound_bridge_zero_dispatch_capacity_blocks_without_retaining_action`
constructs a capacity=0 bridge, processes one event against a
permissive rule, and asserts:
  - actions_dispatched is empty
  - actions_blocked has exactly one entry with reason
    "dispatch_queue_full(capacity=0)"

## 2. storage: rename PaneIndexingStats.fts_row_count → fts_indexed_count

Readability rename that touches a public struct and two call sites.
The old name `fts_row_count` was misleading — FTS5 external-content
tables do NOT expose an accurate row count via SELECT COUNT(*)
because the shadow index rowid space is the segment id space, not
an independent count. The field actually represents "how many
SEGMENTS have been indexed into FTS so far", which `fts_indexed_count`
describes unambiguously.

Rename applied to:
  - struct PaneIndexingStats field
  - doc comment on the `fts_consistent` field
  - build_indexing_health_report's folded sum
  - The trigger-driven assignment: `fts_indexed_count: segment_count`

Test helper `fts_row_count(conn)` in the deferred-FTS test module
renamed to `fts_match_count(conn, match_token)` and rewritten to
use a `WHERE output_segments_fts MATCH ?1` query, which hits the
FTS5 index directly instead of projecting through the external
content table — the old implementation gave the wrong count on
external-content tables.

## 3. mux dispatch: propagate readable-wait errors instead of silencing

In process_async, an error from `wait_for_readable()` was previously
logged and then turned into `Ok(())` — the dispatcher would exit
cleanly as if the client had disconnected normally. That's wrong
for non-disconnect error classes (EIO, ENOMEM, etc.) because callers
would see a clean termination and never investigate the underlying
OS-level failure.

Fix: check `is_clean_disconnect(&err)` first (the same helper used
at dispatch.rs:382 and :808 for the other error-handling call
sites). If the error IS a clean disconnect, return Ok(()) — preserving
the prior behavior for the common case. Otherwise propagate the
error with an explanatory context:

    return Err(err).context("waiting for mux stream readiness or dispatch item");

Regression test `process_async_propagates_readable_wait_failures`
builds a FailingReadableDispatchStream that always returns
io::Error::other("readable wait failed") from wait_for_readable,
drives process_async against it, and asserts:
  - the overall result is Err (not Ok)
  - the error message contains "readable wait failed" so the
    context-wrapping preserves the inner reason

The test stream's AsyncRead and AsyncWrite impls use
`panic!(...)` bodies to ensure poll_read / poll_write NEVER run in
this scenario — the readability wait must fail before any buffer
I/O is attempted.

## Beads

Minor metadata.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"ft-e8brh","title":"[MEDIUM] cancel_races_child_creation test has weak race coverage; passes trivially via propagation","description":"In commit 6a16ae0a (fix(ft-2xnop): add unix umask coverage ... +
cancellation concurrency tests), the test
\`cancel_races_child_creation_all_children_see_cancellation\` at
crates/frankenterm-core/src/cancellation.rs:2635-2676 claims to verify
the cancel+child race but does not reliably exercise the race window.

Structure:
\`\`\`rust
for _ in 0..20 {
    let parent = CancellationToken::new(...);
    let n_creators = 8;
    let barrier = Arc::new(Barrier::new(n_creators + 1));

    let handles: Vec<_> = (0..n_creators).map(|i| {
        let parent = parent.clone();
        let barrier = Arc::clone(&barrier);
        thread::spawn(move || {
            barrier.wait();
            parent.child(ScopeId(format!(\"c_{i}\")))
        })
    }).collect();

    barrier.wait();
    parent.cancel(ShutdownReason::UserRequested);
    // ... assert every child is cancelled
}
\`\`\`

Problems:

1. The barrier synchronizes the START of each thread's work but not
   the duration of \`parent.child(...)\`. On a machine with >=8 cores,
   the 8 creator threads typically finish \`parent.child(...)\` BEFORE
   the main thread reaches \`parent.cancel(...)\`. In that ordering, all
   children are pre-existing when cancel propagates — a trivial case
   covered by other tests in the file.

2. 20 sequential rounds with no scheduler perturbation (no \`yield_now\`,
   no sleep, no thread count above core count) rarely hit the interesting
   interleaving where cancel runs mid-child-creation.

3. The assertion passes even if \`parent.child()\` has a bug where it
   reads the \"parent cancelled\" flag AFTER registration but BEFORE
   propagation reaches it — because the test never creates that window.

Impact: false confidence in race-safe \`CancellationToken::child()\`
under concurrent cancel. A latent bug where a newly-created child
escapes both propagation (not-yet-registered) AND post-cancel detection
(child() reads stale flag) would not be caught.

Fix direction:
- Spawn more creators than CPU cores (e.g., 64) so some are preempted
  mid-call.
- Insert \`thread::yield_now()\` or a short sleep inside the creator
  body before calling \`parent.child()\` to widen the race window.
- Alternatively, use an inner barrier that creators wait on AFTER
  they've decided to create the child but BEFORE registration, so the
  main thread can force the interleaving.
- Consider using loom or shuttle for deterministic interleaving
  coverage instead of probabilistic races.

Severity: medium — test quality issue, not a proven correctness bug
in cancellation.rs. Filing because the test claims coverage it does

 {"id":"ft-fbee5","title":"pattern pack discovery followed symlinks out of tree","description":"# [HIGH] pattern-pack loader: symlink-escape via discover_packs_from_dir

**Subsystem:** patterns / scan_pipeline
**File:** crates/frankenterm-core/src/patterns.rs

## The hole

`load_pack_from_file(path, root)` routes through `sandbox_resolve` only when a
`Some(root)` is supplied. `discover_packs_from_dir`, used for
`.ft/patterns/**` discovery from a workspace root, was calling
`load_pack_from_file(path, None)` for every file + rules.toml it iterated —
so the sandbox check was **skipped** even though the dir itself was
workspace-attacker-controlled.

A hostile repo could ship:

    .ft/patterns/config.yaml -> /Users/victim/.ssh/id_rsa

`path.is_file()` follows the symlink (returns true), `load_pack_from_file`
opens the target, feeds its contents to `serde_yaml::from_str`, and on parse
failure emits:

    tracing::warn!("Skipping invalid user pack {}: {e}", path.display());

Which can echo excerpts of the target file via serde's error reporting
(`mapping expected, found: …`).

## Pre-conditions

* victim clones a hostile repo that contains a symlink under
  `.ft/patterns/`;
* frankenterm loads user packs at session start.

## Fix

`discover_packs_from_dir` now passes `Some(dir)` as the sandbox root to
`load_pack_from_file`. `sandbox_resolve` canonicalizes and rejects any
resolved path outside `dir`. Added regression test
`discover_packs_rejects_symlink_escape`.

## Residual risk

A top-level symlink replacing `.ft/patterns` itself would still escape
because the caller (`load_packs_from_config`) builds `ws_dir =
root_path.join(".ft").join("patterns")` without canonicalizing. That's a
follow-up — tracking separately.

## Sibling work

- 33e84436 security(patterns): add sandbox_resolve helper (helper introduction)
- e8397747 / 8e57c8e0 fix(limits): MAX_PACK_BYTES 16 MiB cap (orthogonal DoS layer)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:26:10.019601Z","created_by":"p3-review-audit","updated_at":"2026-04-21T17:54:22.244142Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:54:22.243799Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 6ab52765 — canonicalize + in-tree check applied to pack discovery","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ft-05hfm","patterns","security"]}

 {"id":"ft-olq2e","title":"[HIGH] integration_mux_checkpoint_topology never validates post-rollback topology","description":"Audit finding from commit 909c3d89.

File: crates/frankenterm-core/tests/integration_mux_checkpoint_topology.rs:385-389

The test claims to verify that rollback restores the original topology, but it reconstructs `restored_topo` from `initial_topo_json` captured before mutation, not from the server/registry state after rollback:

- line 308 serializes the initial topology into `initial_topo_json`
- lines 385-389 deserialize that same baseline JSON and compare it to `initial_topo`

That assertion is vacuous: it will pass even if `RemoteRequest::Rollback` leaves the server in the wrong pane/tree layout, as long as the old serialized baseline is still parseable. The test currently proves JSON roundtrip of the baseline snapshot, not rollback correctness.

Expected fix:
- derive the post-rollback topology from the actual restored server state (or its durable checkpoint payload), then compare pane ids/tree structure against the pre-expansion baseline
- keep the current rollback-history assertions as secondary checks, not the only evidence

 {"id":"ft-qgk35","title":"[mor:security-engineering] wa.send trust boundary is 'which agent', not 'what bytes'","description":"# Observation

`WaSendTool` (mcp_tools.rs:1819) caps payload at 4 MiB (ft-05hfm) and
gates via the policy engine. Everything the authenticated agent
submits as \`text\` is then forwarded through
\`wezterm cli send-text -- <text>\` to the target pane.

The Command-args layering is safe from shell injection: args are
passed directly, not through \`sh -c\`. But send-text is typing
bytes into a terminal, and those bytes include the full control-char
space:

- \\x03 (Ctrl+C) kills the foreground process
- \\x04 (Ctrl+D) sends EOF, often exiting shells/REPLs
- \\x1a (Ctrl+Z) suspends
- Escape sequences can fake prompt text (`\\x1b[H\\x1b[2J$ `)
- Bracketed-paste wrappers can be forged

Trust model today: "if the agent is authenticated and policy allows
scope, arbitrary bytes are fine." This is appropriate for the
normal case (an agent typing \`npm test\` into its own pane). It is
less obviously appropriate for:

- agent A sending into agent B's pane (policy gates scope but not
  content)
- a compromised/misconfigured agent signing off its scope with a
  ghost Ctrl+C
- typing \`\\x1bc\` (reset terminal) into a neighbor pane to mask
  the audit trail of whatever just scrolled off

# Question

Would a lightweight content class — "does this payload contain
control characters outside the normal typing range?" — as a
separate policy capability be worth the complexity? The default
policy could allow control chars (current behavior); a hardening
toggle could require an explicit \`send-control-chars\` capability.

Agents that need to Ctrl+C their own long-running commands would
declare the capability up front; the 99% of typing-plain-text cases
would run without it and be structurally incapable of sending
Ctrl+C into another pane.

# Trust boundary

Current boundary: authenticated identity + policy scope + size cap.
Open question: should the boundary also include "which byte classes
can flow"? For security platforms, the answer is usually yes; the
tradeoff is ergonomics cost during normal agent operation.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"question","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:35:28.713563Z","created_by":"mor-review","updated_at":"2026-04-21T18:02:57.980813Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T18:02:30.134277Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["mcp","mor","policy","security"],"comments":[{"id":3601,"issue_id":"ft-qgk35","author":"content-trust-audit","text":"answered — investigation found content-aware gating already exists via PolicyEngine::analyze_content_risk (policy.rs:4792) + PredicateKind::CommandPattern (policy.rs:3296). Trust boundary IS identity + bytes, not identity alone. Specific extension to cover terminal-control bytes (Ctrl+C/D/Z, ESC, bracketed-paste wraps) filed as child ft-qgk35.1 (P2) with ~10-line patch + 5 tests.","created_at":"2026-04-21T18:02:57Z"}]}
 {"id":"ft-qgk35.1","title":"[security] wa.send: extend analyze_content_risk to flag terminal-control bytes","description":"# Parent

ft-qgk35 asked whether wa.send's trust boundary should include "what
bytes flow," not just "which agent is authenticated." Investigation
found the policy engine **already has content-aware gating** — the
question collapses from "should we add a mechanism?" to "should we
extend the existing mechanism to cover a specific blind spot?"

# Mechanism already in place

`PolicyEngine::analyze_content_risk` (crates/frankenterm-core/src/policy.rs:4792)
runs on every `ActionKind::SendText` with non-empty `command_text`.
It flags:

- **content.destructive_tokens** (40 pts): rm -rf, DROP TABLE, dd if=, …
- **content.sudo_elevation** (30 pts): sudo / doas / run0
- **content.multiline_complex** (15 pts): multi-line heredoc
- **content.pipe_chain** (10 pts): ≥ 2 pipes

And `PredicateKind::CommandPattern { patterns }`
(crates/frankenterm-core/src/policy.rs:3296) lets operators write
regex-match rules on `command_text`.

So content-aware gating **exists**. The parent bead (ft-qgk35)'s
framing of "trust boundary is identity, not bytes" is incorrect —
the boundary already inspects bytes for a specific class of risks.

# The actual gap

`analyze_content_risk` is **shell-command aware** (rm -rf, sudo,
pipes) but not **terminal-control aware**. It does not flag:

- \x03 (Ctrl+C) — can kill another agent's foreground process
- \x04 (Ctrl+D) — can exit another agent's REPL/shell
- \x1a (Ctrl+Z) — can background another agent's work
- \x1b prefix (escape sequences) — can fake prompts, scramble
  the scrollback, clear the screen to mask evidence
- Bracketed-paste wrappers (\x1b[200~ ... \x1b[201~) — can be
  forged to make injected text read as human-pasted by agent
  detection heuristics

On the fleet of 200+ panes described in README.md, a compromised or
misconfigured agent that has scope to wa.send into a neighbor pane
today has unrestricted ability to type control bytes. The risk
surface is not hypothetical — agent A releasing its reservation on
pane B via a ghost Ctrl+C is a known class of coordination failure
in multi-agent terminal systems.

# Proposed tightening

Add a new risk factor to `analyze_content_risk`:

```rust
// Terminal-control bytes
let has_control_byte = text.bytes().any(|b|
    b == 0x03 || b == 0x04 || b == 0x1a  // Ctrl+C, Ctrl+D, Ctrl+Z
    || b == 0x1b                           // ESC (escape sequences)
);
if has_control_byte {
    self.add_factor(
        factors,
        "content.terminal_control_bytes",
        RiskCategory::Content,
        25,  // between multiline (15) and sudo (30)
        "Payload contains terminal-control bytes (Ctrl+C/D/Z or ESC)",
    );
}
```

Point value 25 chosen so default `risk_config.allow_max` still
permits typical interactive use (which should not contain control
bytes; agents that type `npm test` don't include \x03). Agents that
DO need Ctrl+C for long-running workflows trip the factor and
either:
  - pass through (if policy's allow_max accommodates 25), or
  - land in require-approval (if allow_max is below 25).

Either way the event is **visible in the audit trail** — which is
the actual ask from the parent bead.

# Alternative (higher-cost)

Introduce `PaneCapability::AcceptControlBytes` as a separate gate.
Richer but requires:
  - schema change to PaneCapabilities
  - migration for existing PaneState rows
  - config surface for operators to declare which panes accept
    control bytes

The risk-factor approach above is a ~10-line change with no schema
impact; the capability approach is ~150 lines and a migration. Pick
risk-factor unless a future bead explicitly wants per-pane
structural gating.

# Tests that should land with the fix

1. `analyze_content_risk_flags_ctrl_c`: "npm test\x03" triggers
   terminal_control_bytes factor.
2. `analyze_content_risk_plain_text_unchanged`: "npm test" does NOT
   trigger it (false-positive guard on printable ASCII).
3. `analyze_content_risk_flags_esc_prefix`: "\x1bc" (reset
   terminal) triggers.
4. `analyze_content_risk_flags_bracketed_paste_wrap`: the literal
   bytes `\x1b[200~...\x1b[201~` trigger (agent forging a paste).
5. `wa_send_dry_run_reports_control_byte_risk`: end-to-end from
   the MCP tool layer; dry-run with a Ctrl+C payload surfaces the
   factor in the injection preview.

# Disposition on parent

ft-qgk35 can be closed as `answered`: the mechanism exists; this
sub-bead tracks the specific extension. Keeping ft-qgk35 open risks

 {"id":"ft-qhyfh","title":"[LOW][review] user_packs discovery skips sandbox_resolve_dir (ft-6mxfs follow-up)","description":"# Observation

From a review pass over 10 swarm commits in the last 2 hours (git log --since='2 hours ago'), specifically the ft-6mxfs canonicalization fix (0239fbd6).

## Context

ft-6mxfs closed a directory-boundary symlink escape in `.ft/patterns` discovery by routing through `sandbox_resolve_dir(&ws_dir, root_path)` before walking. The fix is correct and well-tested.

## The gap

patterns.rs:938 — when `config.user_packs_enabled = true`, the code takes a different branch:

```rust
if config.user_packs_enabled {
    if let Some(user_dir) = config.resolved_user_packs_dir() {
        let discovered = discover_packs_from_dir(&user_dir)?;
        ...
    }
}
```

This call does NOT route through `sandbox_resolve_dir`. The user-packs dir is either the user-configured `user_packs_dir` (tilde-expanded) or `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/patterns`.

## Threat-model delta

Unlike `.ft/patterns` (inside a cloned repo — attacker-controlled), `~/.config/wa/patterns/` is under the user's own control. So the escape window is narrower — the user would have to set up the symlink themselves, at which point they already have write access.

However:
- If the user shares dotfiles across machines and a symlink in their config dir points at something sensitive on one machine but not another, a stray `wa` invocation could read files outside the intended scope.
- The inner per-file `sandbox_resolve` (ft-fbee5) still provides a per-YAML defense, so this is defense-in-depth, not a primary vulnerability.

## Proposed fix

Call `sandbox_resolve_dir(&user_dir, &user_dir.parent().unwrap_or(&user_dir))` or pass an explicit `user_sandbox_root` so the canonicalized-inside-root invariant extends to the user-packs path. Keep the behavior that a missing directory is Ok(None) (already the common case).

## Review context

Found via multi-pass review after shipping ft-h9g0q follow-up (retry.rs PaneNotFound classification). - Support crates already partially know about native Asupersync:

- The first idea in this bead description about creating an Async<T>-like generic adapter should NOT be the default architecture.
- Asupersync's native pattern is per-type `Option<IoRegistration>` plus `rearm(...)` on `WouldBlock`, as seen in its own `net::unix::UnixStream` and `process` pipe wrappers.

2. SSH proxy / pipe path second.
   files: `frankenterm/client/src/client.rs`, possibly `frankenterm/ssh/*` only if needed.
   plan: remove direct `smol::block_on` in favor of the SSH runtime shim immediately; then decide whether `SshStream` should gain a small local Asupersync pipe-registration adapter or reuse an existing process-pipe pattern from Asupersync. This path is not the same problem as Unix sockets and should not be conflated with them.

3. TLS/OpenSSL path third, coordinated with finish-line networking work.
   files: `frankenterm/client/src/client.rs`, `frankenterm/async_ossl/src/lib.rs`.
   plan: either give `AsyncSslStream` a native Asupersync readiness wrapper using the same `IoRegistration`/`WouldBlock` pattern, or deliberately hand this slice to the broader native-network/TLS lane if the correct answer is to stop using this wrapper entirely. Do not hide TLS behind another executor-neutral shim.

Immediate low-risk cleanup opportunities once the first slice starts

 {"id":"ft-ybtyg","title":"[MEDIUM] session checkpoint + snapshot files have no integrity check; 'BLAKE3' docs vs SipHash reality","description":"Found during /security-audit-for-saas pass #5 (pane 5, 2026-04-21).

## Summary
Two related gaps in the session-persistence trust story:

1. Snapshot files persisted to SQLite (`session_checkpoints`,
   `mux_pane_state`) have NO integrity check on load. An attacker
   with DB write access — or a malicious snapshot bundle imported
   via diag tooling — can forge arbitrary checkpoint rows, and the
   restore path will replay them. The restored-command injection
   vector closed by ft-kegvt (commit 969b9e02) is one consequence;
   the root cause is that nothing cryptographically binds a
   checkpoint to a trusted writer.

2. Documentation drift amplifies the problem: snapshot_engine.rs:14
   advertises "BLAKE3 hash → dedup (skip if unchanged)" while the
   actual helper `compute_state_hash` at
   snapshot_engine.rs:1641-1651 uses
   `std::collections::hash_map::DefaultHasher` (SipHash-24, not a
   cryptographic hash). Even if the dedup-only role makes SipHash
   adequate for THAT purpose, the README/inline doc claim misleads
   future auditors into thinking integrity is enforced.

## Evidence

### Missing integrity verification
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/session_restore.rs:466-474 and
  :509-517: `INSERT INTO session_checkpoints (..., state_hash,
  ...)` with the literal string `'restore'` as the state_hash
  column value. No hash is computed, stored, or later verified.
- storage.rs:609 schema comment claims `state_hash TEXT NOT NULL,
  -- BLAKE3 of serialized state for dedup`, but the INSERT sites
  (:2031, :2268, :2589) either pass a computed SipHash hex string
  (from `compute_state_hash`) or the hardcoded `'restore'`.
- grep confirms zero `verify_snapshot`, `snapshot_hash`,
  `hmac`, `HMAC`, or `verify.*checkpoint` sites across
  `snapshot_engine.rs` + `session_restore.rs`. Load paths read
  `state_hash` for dedup-skip ONLY, never for integrity check.

### Documentation drift
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/snapshot_engine.rs:14 — the module
  docstring says:
      `├── BLAKE3 hash  → dedup (skip if unchanged)`
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/snapshot_engine.rs:1641-1651:
      ```rust
      fn compute_state_hash(panes: &[PaneInfo]) -> String {
          use std::hash::{Hash, Hasher};
          let mut hasher =
              std::collections::hash_map::DefaultHasher::new();
          ...
          ids.hash(&mut hasher);
          ...
          format!("{:016x}", hasher.finish())
      }
      ```
  That's SipHash-24 formatted as a 16-hex-char u64. Not BLAKE3.
  No `blake3::` / `sha2::` imports in snapshot_engine.rs.

## Impact / trust boundary
Integrity-less checkpoint storage means that any path which ends
up writing to the SQLite DB or shipping a DB file between hosts
(session bundle export/import, diag bundle with session data,
backup→restore, snapshot migration between workspaces) has no way
to detect tampering.

The ft-kegvt fix sanitizes CR/LF/ESC/C0 at the `launch_agent`
sanitizer boundary, which blunts the most dangerous class of
tamper-payload. But it doesn't stop an attacker from, e.g.,
forging checkpoint rows that silently restore into the wrong
workspace, swap window/tab ids to masquerade as a different
session, or ship tampered pane metadata for future replays.

Severity: MEDIUM. Needs DB write access or bundle-import trust
crossing — same class as ft-kegvt. Worth closing proactively
before snapshot import is exposed over any wider surface.

## Fix direction

1. Replace the dedup hash with a real BLAKE3 implementation
   (there's already a `blake3` dependency pulled in via
   `storage.rs` + `codec`). Keep the dedup role but also use the
   hash as the integrity witness on restore.
2. Drop the hardcoded `'restore'` placeholder at session_restore.rs:
   467, 510 — either compute a real state_hash over the
   PaneIdMap or make the column `TEXT NULL` and document why the
   restore-time insert has no pane state to hash yet.
3. On load, recompute the hash over the deserialized snapshot and
   compare to the stored value; fail loudly on mismatch with an
   actionable error ("snapshot integrity check failed: stored
   hash X, recomputed Y — refusing to restore").
4. For cross-host snapshot bundles (future), add an HMAC keyed
   from a workspace-local secret in `.ft/hmac.key` so bundles
   cannot be silently swapped between workspaces.
5. Fix the docstring drift at snapshot_engine.rs:14 in lockstep
   with whichever real hash gets adopted.

## Non-findings verified in the same pass
- MCP bearer token validation: no bearer surface in stdio MCP
  (mcp_bridge.rs). Auth is out-of-band via OS process context.
  Clean.
- `wa.send` approval enforcement in non-TTY: mcp_tools.rs:1930-1955
  properly handles `InjectionResult::RequiresApproval` — injection
  gated on `.is_allowed()` at line 1955. Approval-required actions
  return the envelope back, do not auto-bypass. Clean.
- Distributed mode token validation: `constant_time_eq` at
  distributed.rs:209-222 is correctly implemented. `validate_token`
  at :248-289 uses it for both identity and secret. Clean.
- Agent-mail contact handshake: out of scope per AGENTS.md rule
  (NEVER touch agent-mail service). Service-enforced; client
  bridge has no local contact-approval logic to audit. Out of
  scope by policy.

## Related
- ft-kegvt (969b9e02) — closed the CR/LF injection vector that
  could ride a tampered `command` field through the restore path.
  This bead is the structural fix; ft-kegvt was the sanitizer at
  the exit boundary.
- ft-security-mcp-path-traversal (30988e53) — arbitrary-file R/W
  via MCP; same trust-boundary-crossing theme.

 {"id":"ft-yjt9e","title":"policy::RateLimiter::pane_counts leaks entries forever — no eviction on pane destroy or GC path","description":"# Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/policy.rs:2076` — `RateLimiter.pane_counts: HashMap<(u64, ActionKind), Vec<Instant>>`. Every unique `(pane_id, action)` pair `check()` sees creates an entry. Nothing removes them.

A grep confirms it:

    $ rg 'pane_counts\.(remove|clear|retain)|fn gc|fn cleanup|fn prune_panes' crates/frankenterm-core/src/policy.rs
    (no matches)

## Failure shape

On a long-running ft watcher with high pane churn (the 200+-pane fleet the project is designed for), every closed pane leaves a stale `(pane_id, action)` entry in `pane_counts` — one per ActionKind the pane ever touched. Over weeks those entries accumulate:

- Each entry holds a `Vec<Instant>` of up to `limit_per_pane` entries (default 60, configurable up to u32::MAX).
- After the sliding window expires, `prune_old` empties the Vec on the next `check()` — but only if check() is called with that (pane_id, action) again, which never happens for a destroyed pane.
- The entry itself is never removed, so the HashMap grows monotonically.

For 10k destroyed panes × 5 action kinds × 16B-per-Instant × 60-entries-peak = ~48 MB of leaked state. Not a crash, but a silent drift that scales with fleet uptime.

Global side (`global_counts: HashMap<ActionKind, Vec<Instant>>`) is BOUNDED by the finite ActionKind enum — not affected.

# Fix

Add two method shapes to `impl RateLimiter`:

1. `remove_pane(&mut self, pane_id: u64)` — iterate pane_counts, retain only keys whose first element ≠ pane_id. Called from the runtime's PaneDestroyed handler (same handler that already calls `remove_runtime_pane_state_for_pane` in runtime.rs:3574).

2. `gc(&mut self)` (or `gc_at(&mut self, now: Instant)` for testability) — iterate pane_counts, for each entry call prune_old with now-window as cutoff, drop the whole entry if the resulting Vec is empty. Safe opportunistic cleanup that catches panes closed without an explicit PaneDestroyed signal (crashes, abrupt detaches).

Also add operational telemetry: `pane_entries_evicted: u64` counter so operators can see the leak being managed.

# Tests

Inline `#[cfg(test)]` in policy.rs:

- `rate_limiter_remove_pane_drops_all_action_entries_for_that_pane`
- `rate_limiter_remove_pane_leaves_other_panes_intact`
- `rate_limiter_gc_drops_expired_empty_entries`
- `rate_limiter_gc_preserves_still-active_entries`

# Severity

MEDIUM — leak is slow, bounded per pane, doesn't panic. But it's cumulative and silent. Fleet operators would only see the symptom as gradual RSS creep over uptime-weeks, attributed to anything-but-rate-limiter. Shipping the eviction path closes the hole before someone has to bisect it from production.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:51:26.703056Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:57:11.104139Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T10:57:11.103827Z","close_reason":"Landed as part of 27ef45e1 (absorbed commit includes ft-yjt9e methods: RateLimiter::remove_pane, gc, gc_at, tracked_pane_entry_count + 4 regression tests: remove_pane_drops_all_action_entries_for_that_pane, remove_pane_leaves_global_counts_intact, gc_drops_expired_empty_entries, gc_preserves_still_active_entries). Lib compiles clean.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

-{"id":"ft-yskcu","title":"continuous_backpressure EwmaSmoother + sigmoid NaN poison cascade","description":"## Defect

Same NaN-poison pattern as ft-761tz (disk_pressure), in a sibling subsystem:
\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/continuous_backpressure.rs\`.

## Concrete failure path

1. Caller invokes \`ContinuousBackpressure::update(capture_ratio, write_ratio)\` where
   one ratio is \`NaN\`. Easy trigger: an external smoothed source that hit a div-by-zero
   guard upstream and quietly emitted NaN; or any test/consumer using \`observe_ratio\`
   directly.
2. Line 225-226: \`capture_ratio.clamp(0.0, 1.0)\` returns NaN — \`f64::clamp\` propagates
   NaN by design.
3. Line 228: \`self.capture_smoother.update(NaN)\`. \`EwmaSmoother::update\` (line 105)
   has **no NaN guard** — \`alpha.mul_add(NaN, ...)\` → NaN → \`self.value = NaN\`
   permanently. Every subsequent \`update()\` call preserves the NaN.
4. Line 231: \`smoothed_capture.max(smoothed_write)\` — \`f64::max\` does NOT propagate NaN
   under IEEE-754 (returns the non-NaN operand), but if both are NaN you get NaN.
5. Line 232-236: \`severity(NaN, _, _)\` → calls local \`sigmoid\` (line 140) which does
   \`x.clamp(-500.0, 500.0)\` — same NaN-propagation footgun. Returns NaN.
6. Line 232: \`self.current_severity = NaN\` permanently.
7. \`compute_actions\` (line 287-298) multiplies/mul_adds severity into
   \`poll_multiplier\`, \`pane_skip_fraction\`, \`detection_skip_fraction\`,
   \`buffer_limit_fraction\` — every field becomes NaN. ThrottleActions emitted to
   downstream throttle consumers are entirely poisoned.
8. \`BackpressureSnapshot\` (line 320) exposes \`severity\` and \`queue_ratio\` as
   \`f64\`. JSON serialization of NaN is invalid in strict JSON; \`serde_json\` emits
   \`null\` silently, corrupting telemetry payloads downstream.
9. Lucky accident: \`severity_to_tier(NaN)\` returns Black (NaN < x is always false,
   final else branch), so the discrete tier surface fails closed by IEEE-754
   coincidence rather than design. The continuous severity surface fails OPEN.

## Severity rationale

- Same class of silent wrong answer as ft-761tz.
- Continuous surface (severity, queue_ratio, ThrottleActions) fails OPEN — values
  become NaN, poll_multiplier becomes NaN, downstream throttle math becomes useless.
- Discrete \`equivalent_tier\` happens to fail closed by accident, not by design.
- Telemetry consumers (snapshot serialization) silently lose data.

## Fix sketch

1. \`EwmaSmoother::update\` (line 105) — if \`raw.is_nan()\`, return \`self.value\`
   without state change. Same defense as disk_pressure \`EwmaEstimator::update\`.
2. Local \`sigmoid\` (line 140) — short-circuit \`is_nan()\` to \`0.5\` like the
   backpressure_severity sibling does.
3. \`ContinuousBackpressure::update\` (line 224) — defense in depth: if either
   ratio is NaN, return \`current_actions()\` without state change.
4. Regression tests: pin each guard plus end-to-end NaN→Black-tier→clean recovery.

## Scope

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/continuous_backpressure.rs\` only. No cross-crate API
changes. No changes to \`backpressure.rs\` or \`backpressure_severity.rs\`
(both already hardened).","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane0","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:05:30.189872Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:05:34.055242Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-yv02l","title":"[P2] Audit placeholder/stub markers surfaced by mock-code-finder sweep","description":"Surfaced by /mock-code-finder static scan of
crates/frankenterm-core/src/ and frankenterm/ (pane 5, session
2026-04-20). Seven findings; none reach ship-blocking severity, but
each warrants a targeted review to either remove the placeholder,
document the invariant that makes it safe, or replace with a real
implementation.

Categories:
  1. TODO/FIXME/XXX in production
  2. unimplemented!() stubs
  3. placeholder values overwritten later
  4. magic fallback values masking platform/error conditions

Findings:

- crates/frankenterm-core/src/telemetry.rs:242 (cat 4)
  `collect_system_memory` returns `(0, 0)` on non-Linux/non-macOS.
  Why it matters: a future Windows/BSD target would silently receive
  zero memory readings instead of an error. Consider returning
  `Result<(u64, u64), _>` or documenting the platform contract.

- crates/frankenterm-core/src/runtime_compat.rs:1473 (cat 1)
  `// TODO(asupersync): replace with asupersync::select! or ...`
  Why it matters: the only surviving prod TODO in frankenterm-core;
  pane 6 owns the asupersync migration, already tracked by
  ft-xbnl0.2.x epic.

- crates/frankenterm-core/src/user_preferences.rs:729 (cat 3)
  `action: UserAction::Ignore, // placeholder` inside the synthetic
  trajectory generator. Why it matters: value is overwritten a few
  lines below via `best_action`, but the placeholder is still
  exposed via `obs_base.action` during reward computation between
  lines 730-755. Review whether any feature extractor reads the
  placeholder value.

- crates/frankenterm-core/src/search/vector_index.rs:126 (cat 3)
  `// placeholder for count — will be patched on finish`. Why it
  matters: if the finish path ever takes an early-return before
  patching, the placeholder ships. Add an assertion at finish.

- crates/frankenterm-core/src/dataflow.rs:980 (cat 3)
  `kind: NodeKind::Source, // placeholder`. Why it matters: same
  pattern as vector_index — review the constructor exit paths.

- frankenterm/mux/src/tab.rs:4078,4082,4090,4098,4106,4110,4114,
  4141,4144,4150,4158,4161,4164,4170 (cat 2) — 14 `unimplemented!()`
  calls. Why it matters: the cluster needs domain-owner review;
  pane 1 (mux) territory.

- frankenterm/mux/src/pane.rs:609,612,616,624,670,674 (cat 2) — 6
  `unimplemented!()` calls. Why it matters: same cluster as tab.rs,
  pane 1 territory.

No defect shipped from this sweep because the findings are either
low-severity (telemetry fallback on unsupported platforms), tracked
elsewhere (runtime_compat TODO → ft-xbnl0.2.x), or owned by other
panes (mux unimplemented stubs).

Related: in the prior ticks this session I shipped two defensive
fixes for real panic-on-input defects surfaced by similar scans —

 {"id":"ft-ywlpu","title":"orphan_reaper misparses wezterm cli flag-value forms before subcommand","description":"parse_ps_line_if_reapable() finds the first non-flag token after `cli` and treats it as the subcommand.
That rejects valid invocations like `wezterm cli --config-file /tmp/x.toml list`, where `/tmp/x.toml` is a flag value, not the subcommand.
Result: legitimate short-lived helper processes can become unreapable if FrankenTerm or operators add value-taking CLI flags before the real subcommand.
Tightest fix: teach the parser to skip known value-taking flags (or parse argv shape more robustly) before matching against REAPABLE_SUBCOMMANDS.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T02:59:49.563841Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T03:23:51.880516Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T03:23:51.880272Z","close_reason":"Parser now accepts wezterm cli value-taking flags before reapable subcommands; compiled allowlist check fixed.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
